title: Beef Stew with Herb Dumplings
subtitle: Slow-braised beef in a rich gravy, with herbed suet dumplings cooked in the pot
excerpt: Beef stew with herb dumplings is a two-part dish: a long braise of braising steak in a deep beef stock gravy, and suet dumplings dropped in for the final 30 minutes. The dumplings absorb the stew's gravy as they cook, becoming soft inside and slightly set on the outside.
sourceNotes: British regional tradition. Suet dumplings in a beef stew are documented across British regional cookbooks from the 18th century and remain in continuous production in domestic and pub cooking. The combination is one of the most characteristically British winter dishes.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the stew without the dumplings. Thaw overnight, reheat, and make fresh dumplings on the day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the stew up to 2 days ahead. Reheat and add dumplings 30 minutes before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small ball of lard or suet-enriched dough cooked on top of a simmering stew. Dumplings absorb steam and cooking liquid, swelling into soft, pillowy rounds with a slightly set exterior. They need the pot covered for the first 15 minutes and uncovered for the last 10 to set the surface.

content.text: Beef stew with dumplings divides its timing into two parts: the long, slow braise of the beef, and the final 25 minutes for the dumplings. The dumplings are the simple part; the stew is the patient part. The gravy needs two hours to reduce from thin stock into something that coats the meat. The dumplings can be made in five minutes.

Heat the oven to 160°C. Season the {{braising-steak}} of beef and toss in the {{plain-flour}} of flour. Brown in batches in a hot frying pan with oil for 3 to 4 minutes per batch. Transfer to a casserole.

In the same pan, fry the {{onion}} onion and {{carrot}} carrot for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and tomato puree. Stir for 1 minute, then tip into the casserole. Add the thyme, bay, Worcestershire sauce, and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. Season. Cover and braise in the oven for 1.5 to 2 hours until the beef is completely tender.

When the beef is tender, mix the dumpling flour, lard, baking powder, parsley, and a generous pinch of salt in a bowl. Add enough cold water, a tablespoon at a time, to bring together into a soft, slightly sticky dough. Divide into 12 equal balls.
Remove the stew from the oven and bring it to a gentle simmer on the hob. Place the
on top of the stew, spacing them apart. Cover and cook over a low heat for 15 minutes. Remove the lid and cook for a further 10 minutes until the dumplings are puffed and set. Serve immediately.

items.cause: Dough was over-worked, or too much water made them heavy.
items.fix: Mix the dumpling dough until just combined, no more. Add water gradually until the dough comes together; it should be soft and sticky, not wet.

items.cause: Pot was not at a steady simmer when they went in, or lid removed too early.
items.fix: The stew must be simmering actively before the dumplings go on. Keep the lid on for the first 15 minutes to trap the steam that cooks them through.

items.symptom: Beef is still tough after 2 hours
items.cause: Oven temperature too low or the lid was not tight.
items.fix: Return to the oven with the lid on for another 30 minutes. Braising steak needs at least 1.5 hours at 160°C to become tender.

content.text: Suet dumplings in a beef stew represent one of the most distinctly British combinations in winter cooking. The dumpling is closely related to the suet crust used in steak and kidney pudding: both use grated hard fat mixed with flour and baking powder to produce a light, yielding dough when cooked. In the stew, the dumplings do double duty as both a bread-substitute and a means of absorbing the gravy. The herb version, with parsley, is the standard; some regional versions use thyme or chives.
